title: Planing a door to fit
subtitle: Take the binding edge back until the door swings free
excerpt: How to diagnose where a door is binding and take the excess timber off with a hand plane: identifying the binding point, removing the door, planing the correct edge, and re-hanging the door to check.
sourceNotes: Newey and Drage, Practical Carpentry and Joinery (c. 1905). Collins Complete DIY Manual.

recipeTools.notes: Useful for end grain on the top or bottom edge.

aliases: how to plane a door edge

glossaryTerms.definition: The latch edge of a door, the vertical edge opposite the hinge edge. The face edge is the one usually planed when a door binds against the frame in the latch area.

content.text: Doors bind for two reasons: the door has swollen (common after wet weather, particularly in period houses with solid timber doors), or the frame has moved. Both are diagnosed the same way: find the binding point, take off the minimum amount of timber to clear it, rehang, and re-paint or re-seal the planed edge to prevent the problem recurring.
Diagnosing the binding point

Close the door until it binds. The shiny or compressed area on the door edge or on the frame rebate is the binding point. Slide a piece of card around the door gap: it should pass freely around the full perimeter. Where the card sticks is the tight point. A door that binds at the top latch corner is the most common pattern; the frame has racked slightly.
Removing the door
Step 1, remove the door from its hinges.

Support the door under the latch edge with a wedge or an assistant. Remove the hinge screws from the door-face side of each hinge. Lift the door clear. For heavy solid-core doors, carry with a second person; door-weight alone can cause the hinge knuckle to jam.
Planing the edge
Step 2, support the door for planing.

Rest the door on its long edge (the latch edge) leaning against a wall, or clamp it in a Workmate (portable bench vice). The plane works best moving from the broader surface down toward the edge; planing the door on its edge in a vice is the standard method.
Step 3, plane the
face edge

Set the plane to a fine cut (a thin shaving). Work along the full edge, not just at the binding area, or a hollow will develop. Take several passes across the full length before returning to the door to test the fit. Remove barely more than the binding point tells you to; it is much easier to take another pass than to re-hang a door with too large a gap.
What a correct result looks like

When rehung, the door should close with light resistance and a clear, consistent gap of 2 to 3 mm on the latch edge and at the top. The gap should taper to a smaller clearance at the hinge side. A heavier, consistent resistance around the full perimeter means the problem is humidity, not a mechanical fit issue; let the door dry out before planing.
Sealing the planed surface

A planed edge that is left bare will re-absorb moisture and swell again. Seal the planed area with two coats of the same paint or varnish as the rest of the door, extending slightly past the planed zone. For unpainted solid timber doors, a coat of linseed oil or Danish oil on the planed edge seals it adequately.
Common mistakes
Planing only at the binding point.
Creates a hollow on the edge that closes the gap too much at the affected area and leaves the gap elsewhere unchanged. Always plane the full length.
Planing the hinge edge when the latch edge is binding.

Re-hanging a door with the hinge edge planed changes the hinge geometry. Plane the latch edge for a latch-side bind; plane the hinge edge only if the bind is at the hinge side and re-shimming the hinge will not fix it.
Not re-sealing the edge.
The problem will recur within one wet season if the planed edge is left bare.
